skill.name: ddd-context-mapping
skill.description: Map relationships between bounded contexts and define integration contracts using DDD context mapping patterns.
skill.summary: Map relationships between bounded contexts and define integration contracts using DDD context mappin...
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings were reviewed against the skill text and dismissed as false positives. Markdown backticks were misclassified as shell execution, and DDD documentation text was misclassified as weak cryptography. No prompt injection, exfiltration, executable code, or unsafe tool behavior was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Misread as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution, but the evidence is Markdown syntax. Line 31 references a local documentation file using inline code formatting, and lines 42-45 show a fenced text example. No executable command is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged characters are part of Markdown inline code and a fenced text block. There is no Ruby code, shell interpolation, command invocation, or execution path in the file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: DDD Prose Misread as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptographic algorithm indicators at documentation lines. The reviewed lines contain skill metadata, scope guidance, output instructions, and limitations for domain-driven design context mapping. No cryptographic API, hash algorithm, encryption call, or security-sensitive implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are plain documentation about DDD context mapping. I found no evidence of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or any other cryptographic implementation.

content.value_statement: Teams often struggle to define ownership and integration rules between bounded contexts. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through DDD context maps, contracts, translation rules, and coupling risks.
actual_capabilities: Identifies bounded context pairs and their dependency direction.
Selects DDD relationship patterns such as customer-supplier, conformist, and anti-corruption layer.
Defines contract ownership for upstream and downstream teams.
Documents translation rules between local domain models.
Highlights coupling risks, failure modes, fallback behavior, and versioning policy.
Uses a reference template for structured context map output.
limitations: Does not design detailed API schemas or data contracts.
Does not replace team negotiation about ownership and priorities.
Requires accurate context names, dependencies, and business boundaries from the user.
Should be revisited when teams, services, or domain ownership change.

use_cases.title: Plan Service Integrations
use_cases.description: Map how product, billing, identity, and fulfillment contexts depend on each other before implementation begins.

use_cases.title: Protect Domain Models During Migration
use_cases.description: Define anti-corruption layers when a legacy system must feed a newer bounded context.

use_cases.title: Clarify Contract Ownership
use_cases.description: Document which team owns each contract, translation rule, failure mode, and versioning policy.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Context Map
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner mapping for a small system.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ddd-context-mapping to map Checkout, Billing, Inventory, and Identity. Show dependency direction, relationship pattern, contract owner, and translation needs.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Integration Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ddd-context-mapping to evaluate these context pairs: Orders to Payments, Orders to Shipping, and Support to Customers. Recommend DDD patterns and explain the tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Design an Anti-Corruption Layer
prompt_templates.scenario: Migration from a legacy upstream system.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ddd-context-mapping to define an anti-corruption layer between Legacy Billing and Subscription Management. Include translation rules, ownership, failure modes, and test needs.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Complex Context Map
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ddd-context-mapping to review this proposed context map for hidden coupling, unclear ownership, missing published language, weak fallback behavior, and risky versioning assumptions.

output_examples.input: Map Checkout, Billing, Inventory, and Fraud for an ecommerce platform.
output_examples.output: A relationship map showing Checkout as downstream of Billing and Inventory, with Fraud using a customer-supplier pattern. The output includes contract owners, translation needs, coupling risks, and mitigation actions.

output_examples.input: Plan a migration from Legacy CRM into a new Customer Profile context.
output_examples.output: An anti-corruption layer plan that protects the Customer Profile model, defines translated terms, assigns contract ownership, lists fallback behavior, and recommends contract tests.

output_examples.input: Review whether Shared Kernel is appropriate between Catalog and Pricing.
output_examples.output: A tradeoff assessment that compares Shared Kernel with published language and customer-supplier patterns, then recommends the option with lower ownership and release risk.

best_practices: Start with real bounded contexts and explicit dependency direction.
Assign a clear owner for every contract and translation rule.
Review the map whenever ownership, business language, or integration behavior changes.
anti_patterns: Using one shared model across contexts without ownership rules.
Choosing an anti-corruption layer without defining concrete translation rules.
Treating the context map as final after teams or service boundaries change.

faq.question: What does this skill produce?
faq.answer: It produces a DDD context map with relationship patterns, contract owners, translation decisions, coupling risks, and mitigation plans.

faq.question: Can it design my API schema?
faq.answer: No. It can identify ownership and translation needs, but detailed API schema design is outside its scope.

faq.question: Does it support anti-corruption layer planning?
faq.answer: Yes. It helps define where an ACL is needed, which terms must be translated, and where boundary code should live.

faq.question: What information should I provide?
faq.answer: Provide context names, known dependencies, team ownership, integration pain points, and any migration or versioning constraints.

faq.question: Is this only for microservices?
faq.answer: No. It applies to any system with separate bounded contexts, even when the implementation is not service based.

faq.question: How should teams use the result?
faq.answer: Use it as an architecture discussion artifact, then confirm ownership, contracts, and tradeoffs with the responsible teams.
